starting_small.i_will_first_action: I will start scheduling a 10-minute check-in at the beginning of any group work session (whether it’s for school, work, or with my family) to confirm who is doing what, what “done” looks like, and when we’ll review progress. I’ve noticed that we often jump straight into tasks and then realize later that we had different assumptions, so I want to make this brief check-in a consistent habit.
starting_small.i_will_second_action: I will make it a point to verbally recognize at least one specific contribution from each teammate during or right after we finish a task together. For example, instead of just saying “good job, everyone,” I’ll say something like, “Alex, your outline really helped us stay focused,” or “Maria, thanks for staying late to double-check the numbers.” I tend to appreciate people silently, but I want to turn that into clear, spoken appreciation.
starting_small.i_will_third_action: I will keep a simple running to-do list for group work with clear checkboxes and due dates, and I will share it with the team (e.g., via Google Docs or a shared note). I’ll update it during and after meetings so everyone can see what is still in progress and what has been completed, instead of relying on my memory or scattered messages.

reflections.reflections_understanding_of_tasks_scope:
The last time I worked in a team was for a group presentation in my evening business communication class at the community college. Our assignment was to create a 15-minute presentation on a local small business and analyze its marketing strategy. The team had four people, and we had about three weeks from the time the assignment was given to the presentation date.

At the very beginning, I understood the general aim of the project—we needed to research a business, gather data, and present recommendations—but I didn’t fully understand the exact scope of my tasks or the timeline until our second meeting. During the first meeting, roles were assigned somewhat quickly: I volunteered to handle background research on the business and to design the slides. However, we did not clearly define what “background research” should include (for example, whether I was also responsible for customer reviews, financial estimates, or just basic company history), and we never wrote down any intermediate deadlines.

Because of that, I initially assumed I only needed to gather basic information: when the business started, what services they provide, who their main customers are, and what their current social media presence looks like. It wasn’t until our instructor mentioned in passing that we should include competitor analysis and basic SWOT elements that I realized my part needed to be more in-depth. At that point, we were already a week and a half into the project.

After some confusion and a few overlapping efforts (another teammate also started doing some of the same research I was doing), we finally created a simple timeline in a shared Google Doc. I wish we had done that from day one. Once the timeline was laid out—with specific dates for finishing research, drafting slides, rehearsing, and final edits—I felt much clearer about what I needed to deliver and by when. But I would say that, overall, my understanding of the tasks and scope was only about 50% clear at the start and only became fully clear after we had already lost some time.
reflections.reflections_results_recognized_and_accom:
In the end, our presentation went quite well. Our group received a 92%, and the instructor specifically mentioned that our visuals were clean and easy to follow, and that our recommendations for improving the business’s Instagram presence were practical and clearly explained.

Within the team, I did feel that my contributions were recognized, especially around the slide design. After we finished presenting, one teammate, Jordan, turned to me and said, “Your slides made everything come together. I think that’s why we seemed so organized up there.” Another teammate, Priya, also thanked me in our group chat later that evening for staying up late the night before to fix a few formatting issues and re-order some of the content.

From the instructor’s side, the feedback we received as a group highlighted the strength of the visuals and the clear structure of the presentation. Even though the feedback was addressed to the whole team and not to me individually, I still felt a sense of accomplishment because I knew that those were areas I had put a lot of time into. That said, part of me did wish that the grading rubric or comments had a small space for individual contributions, because I spent significantly more hours working on the slides and integrating everyone’s content than I think was obvious.

Emotionally, I did feel proud when we sat down after presenting. I’m usually a little anxious about public speaking, but seeing how smoothly our slides transitioned and how well the story flowed gave me a quiet sense of satisfaction. I remember thinking, “Okay, the late nights were worth it.” It wasn’t a big celebration or anything, but there was a shared feeling of relief and success among us. I also felt proud that I managed to coordinate everyone’s edits without getting frustrated, which is something I’ve struggled with in other group projects.
reflections.reflections_efforts_recognized_even_with:
There were a few areas where our effort didn’t fully show up in the final result, and the recognition there was more subtle. For example, I spent a good amount of time early on trying to schedule an in-person visit to the business we chose, but the owner kept rescheduling, and eventually we had to rely mostly on online information and a brief phone call instead of a full in-person interview.

Within the team, my effort to set up that visit was acknowledged in our group chat. When the owner cancelled for the second time, Priya wrote, “Thanks for trying so hard to make that happen, it would’ve been great to talk to them directly.” That small comment made me feel like the time I’d spent emailing back and forth and adjusting my schedule wasn’t completely invisible, even though it didn’t produce the result we wanted.

However, from the instructor’s viewpoint, that effort was basically invisible. We didn’t really mention the failed attempts in our presentation because we didn’t want to sound like we were making excuses. As a result, the fact that we didn’t have direct quotes or original photos from the business might have looked like a lack of effort, even though we had actually tried to arrange it.

Another example is that I spent some time experimenting with a short video clip to include in the slides (a quick montage of the business’s storefront and social media screenshots). I ended up not using it because it made the presentation feel a bit too long and busy. No one saw that extra effort except me, and I didn’t really talk about it. In hindsight, I realize that I tend to do a lot of “behind-the-scenes” work that gets cut for the sake of clarity, and I don’t always share that process with the team.

So, inside the team, I’d say my efforts—successful or not—were mostly recognized through quick comments and thank-yous in messages. But in terms of formal recognition (like grading or written feedback), only the visible outputs were acknowledged, not the attempts that didn’t pan out. I think that’s pretty normal, but it did make me aware that if I want certain efforts to be recognized, I might need to communicate them more openly instead of assuming people just know what I tried to do.
reflections.reflections_incomplete_tasks_and_complet:
We had a few tasks that were either incomplete or kept shifting as we realized we needed more or less detail. At the beginning, we did not have a clear system for tracking what was done versus what was still in progress. Tasks were mostly discussed verbally or in our WhatsApp group, which made it easy to lose track.

About halfway through the project, I suggested we create a shared Google Doc with a simple checklist. We listed tasks like: “Background research on business,” “Competitor analysis,” “Customer review summary,” “Slide design,” “Speaker notes,” and “Rehearsal.” Next to each task we wrote the person responsible and the target date. For a task to be considered complete, we agreed that the person responsible had to:
1) Mark it as “DONE” in the document, and
2) Share the file or content in our group chat so others could review it.

Incomplete tasks were handled in a few different ways. For example, Jordan was responsible for competitor analysis, but he was juggling work shifts and fell behind. We noticed a few days before the rehearsal date that his section wasn’t fully ready. Instead of blaming him, we decided to split the remaining work: I took on gathering a few key stats from competitors’ websites, and Priya summarized customer reviews for both our chosen business and two competitors. We then marked the original task in the document as partially complete and created two new sub-tasks with our names. This helped us see concretely what was left.

We also used our rehearsal as a kind of marker of completion. The rule we finally agreed on (a bit late, but still helpful) was that by the time we did a full run-through, all content had to be in the slides and all speaker notes drafted. Anything that wasn’t ready by then was considered a red flag. When we did our rehearsal two days before the real presentation, we noticed that some transitions between sections were still rough, and a couple of slides had too much text. We treated those as “incomplete” in terms of quality, even though technically the tasks were “done.”

The final marker of completion was the moment we exported the slides to PDF and uploaded them to the course portal the night before the presentation. We set a hard cut-off time of 10:00 PM, after which we would not make any more changes unless there was a serious mistake. When we hit that time and everyone confirmed they were okay with the final version, it felt like a clear finish line. Looking back, I realize that having explicit markers like checkboxes, rehearsal dates, and final upload deadlines helped us move from a vague sense of “we’re almost there” to a shared understanding of “we are actually done now.”
reflections.reflection_on_own_role_communication_rec:
In this project, I naturally fell into a kind of informal coordinator role, even though we didn’t officially name a leader. I was the one who created the shared Google Doc, kept track of who was supposed to do what, and reminded people of upcoming dates. I also took on slide design and some of the integration work, combining everyone’s text into a consistent format.

In terms of communication, I think I did fairly well with written updates but not as well with real-time conversations. I regularly posted summaries in our WhatsApp group after we met, like, “Here’s what we decided today: Alex – research; Jordan – competitors; Priya – customer reviews; Me – slides + background; rehearsal on Tuesday at 7 PM.” That helped keep everyone aligned. However, I sometimes avoided bringing up concerns directly when I felt someone was falling behind, because I didn’t want to sound bossy or critical. For instance, when Jordan’s competitor analysis was late, I waited a couple of days before checking in with him, even though I already suspected he might be overwhelmed.

Regarding recognizing others’ accomplishments and efforts, I did try to say thank you and point out specifics. When Priya stayed up late to polish her speaking notes and practiced her part several times, I told her that her confidence really helped set the tone for the rest of us. I also complimented Alex for his clear explanation of the marketing funnel, which became a key part of our presentation. Still, I think my recognition was mostly one-on-one and private rather than in front of the whole group, which might have reduced its impact.

For ensuring completion, I took on a lot of last-minute editing and formatting work. I merged different writing styles, fixed typos, and adjusted slide layouts. While that helped us meet the deadline and present a polished final product, it also meant I carried more stress than I needed to. I didn’t always delegate or ask for help with those final touches because I assumed it would be faster to just do it myself.

Looking back, there are a few things I could have done differently:
- I could have pushed for a clearer division of tasks and written deadlines from the very first meeting, instead of waiting until we were already a bit confused.
- I could have checked in earlier and more openly when I noticed someone was struggling, framing it as, “How can we rebalance the workload?” instead of silently hoping they would catch up.
- I could have celebrated small milestones more, like when we finished our first draft or after the rehearsal, to boost morale and make everyone feel more appreciated.
- I also could have shared my own workload more transparently. Instead of quietly fixing everything late at night, I could have posted in the group, “I’m going to clean up the formatting tonight—if anyone has 20 minutes to double-check spelling or slide numbers, that would help.”

Overall, I was a reliable and organized team member, but I tended to absorb extra work and avoid difficult conversations. In future projects, I want to keep the positive parts—being dependable and detail-oriented—while improving at setting boundaries, asking for help, and giving more visible recognition to others in front of the group.

what_could_have_been_done_differently.what_could_have_been_done_differently:
Looking back on the project, there are several things we could have done differently that would have made the experience smoother and possibly improved our final outcome.

First, we should have invested more time at the very beginning in clarifying the scope and creating a shared plan. Our first meeting was mostly spent deciding which business to choose and casually assigning tasks, but we didn’t actually document our decisions or define what “good enough” looked like for each person’s part. If we had spent even 30 extra minutes agreeing on specific deliverables (for example, “2–3 paragraphs of company history, 3 main competitors with 2 data points each, 10–15 customer reviews summarized into key themes”), we would have avoided some of the overlap and last-minute scrambling.

Second, we could have established regular, short check-ins instead of relying on long, irregular meetings. We had two long in-person meetings and then a lot of scattered messages. A weekly 20-minute video call or even a quick group voice note check-in might have helped us catch issues earlier—like when Jordan was falling behind on competitor research or when I realized we needed more data for the SWOT analysis. By the time we noticed these gaps, we were already close to our rehearsal date.

Third, we might have benefited from being more honest about our schedules and limitations from the start. All of us were juggling work, family, and other classes, but we didn’t explicitly talk about peak busy times or unavailable days. For instance, I work part-time at a bookstore and have my kids on certain evenings, which made some meeting times stressful for me. If we had openly shared our constraints, we could have distributed the workload in a way that matched each person’s availability, instead of assuming everyone had the same amount of free time.

Fourth, we could have made better use of simple tools like a shared calendar or a project management app (even something basic like Trello). We ended up using a Google Doc and WhatsApp, which helped, but our reminders were still somewhat manual. Automated reminders for due dates or a visual board showing task progress might have reduced the mental load on me as the unofficial coordinator and given everyone a clearer picture of where we stood.

Finally, we could have built in time specifically for feedback and revision, not just content creation. Our rehearsal did function as a form of feedback, but it was very close to the final deadline. If we had scheduled an earlier review point—say, a week before the presentation—we could have made deeper improvements to our arguments and storytelling instead of mostly tweaking formatting and timing.

Overall, the main theme is that we treated planning as something secondary to the “real work,” when in fact better planning and more structured communication would have made the real work easier and less stressful.
what_could_have_been_done_differently.type_of_conversation_that_would_have_mad:
Two main types of conversations would have significantly improved our team experience: a structured planning conversation at the start and honest, supportive check-in / feedback conversations in the middle.

1) Structured planning conversation (at the very beginning):
We really needed a dedicated, intentional planning meeting right after the assignment was given. In that conversation, we could have:
- Clarified expectations: Discuss what the instructor was actually looking for, review the rubric together, and agree on what “A-level work” meant for us.
- Defined roles and deliverables: Go around the group and ask, “What are you comfortable doing? What do you want to learn?” Then match tasks to strengths and interests, and write down specific deliverables for each person.
- Created a timeline: Mark out key dates—research completion, first draft of slides, rehearsal date, and final edit. We could have used a shared calendar or at least put it all in the Google Doc in a clearly visible timeline format.
- Set communication norms: Decide how often we’d check in, which platform we’d use (e.g., WhatsApp vs. email), and how quickly we’d aim to respond to messages.

2) Honest, supportive check-in and feedback conversations (mid-project):
Halfway through, we would have benefited from a more open conversation about how things were really going. Instead of just asking, “Is everyone okay with their part?” we could have asked more specific questions like:
- “Is anyone feeling behind or overwhelmed? What can we shift or share?”
- “Are there any parts of the project that feel confusing or unclear?”
- “Does anyone feel like they’re doing more or less than their fair share?”

This kind of conversation would have helped us notice earlier that Jordan was struggling with his schedule and that I was quietly taking on extra integration work. It would also have given us a chance to redistribute tasks in a way that felt fair and sustainable.

Additionally, a gentle feedback conversation after the project would have been helpful. Something like a short debrief where we talk about what went well and what we’d change next time. Not in a blaming way, but in a reflective way: “Next time, let’s do X differently.” We did this informally over text, but a short, intentional debrief could have solidified the lessons and made us more effective for future group work.
